title: Knowledge Distillation
summary: Train smaller student to mimic larger teacher's soft output distributions. Temperature T softens teacher outputs to reveal inter-class structure. Compresses the function, not the weights.
key_insights: Teacher's soft outputs (e.g., 'cat' 0.7, 'dog' 0.2, 'pet' 0.1) richer than hard labels ('cat')
Student trained on teacher's soft outputs via KL divergence + standard CE on hard labels
Temperature T softens distribution: softmax(logits/T) — higher T = more informative
Student architecture can differ completely from teacher
Compresses the function (input→output mapping), not the weight structure
Key for edge deployment and cost reduction

mathematical_core.student_loss: L = α · T² · KL(P_teacher || P_student) + (1-α) · CE(y_true, P_student)
mathematical_core.temperature: T > 1 softens distributions, revealing inter-class relationships

derivations.title: Why soft labels carry more information than hard labels

steps.name: Hard label
steps.math: y = [0, 0, 1, 0, ...] — one-hot. Information content: log(V) bits (which class). No information about relationships between classes.

steps.name: Soft label from teacher
steps.math: P = [0.01, 0.05, 0.70, 0.15, 0.09, ...]. Information: not just 'class 3' but also 'class 4 is somewhat similar (0.15), class 5 less so (0.09), class 1 is very different (0.01)'. This encodes the teacher's learned similarity structure.

steps.name: Information bound
steps.math: Entropy of soft labels H(P) > 0 = H(one-hot). The soft distribution has higher entropy = more information per training example. Student learns from fewer examples because each example teaches more.

derivations.consequence: Soft labels transfer the teacher's learned metric over the output space. The student doesn't just learn 'what' but also 'what else is similar' — a richer training signal.

derivations.title: Temperature scaling — why T² appears in the loss

steps.name: Softmax with temperature
steps.math: P_i = e^{z_i/T} / Σ_j e^{z_j/T}. As T→∞: P → uniform (1/V). As T→0: P → one-hot. T=1: standard softmax.

steps.name: Gradient magnitudes
steps.math: ∂KL/∂z_student scales as 1/T² (because both teacher and student softmax are divided by T). Without compensation, gradients from distillation loss vanish for large T.

steps.name: T² correction
steps.math: Multiply distillation loss by T² to restore gradient magnitude. L = α · T² · KL(P_T_teacher || P_T_student) + (1-α) · CE(y, P_1_student). The T² exactly compensates the 1/T² gradient shrinkage.

derivations.consequence: The T² factor is not arbitrary — it's a gradient normalization. Without it, the distillation signal becomes negligible for T >> 1, and the student only learns from hard labels.

derivations.title: When distillation outperforms training from scratch
derivations.math: Student trained from scratch: learns from N examples, each providing ~log(V) bits (hard labels). Student via distillation: learns from N examples, each providing ~H(P_teacher) bits (soft labels). Effective dataset size ratio: H(P_teacher)/log(V). For V=50000 and teacher entropy ≈ 5 bits: ratio ≈ 5/16.6 ≈ 0.3. BUT the 5 bits are more 'useful' bits (similarity structure vs just class identity).
derivations.consequence: Distillation is most beneficial when: (1) teacher is much larger than student, (2) output space is large (many classes), (3) classes have meaningful similarity structure the teacher has learned.

geometric_properties: Teacher's soft labels encode a learned metric over the output space
Distillation transfers this metric to the student — student's embedding space inherits teacher's similarity structure
Monitor: cosine similarity between teacher and student embeddings for same input — should be high after distillation
statistical_properties: Temperature T controls the entropy of the teaching signal
Typical T: 2-20. Higher T = softer, more informative but noisier signal
α (mixing weight) typically 0.5-0.9 — more weight on distillation loss than hard labels
numerical_properties: Distillation requires running teacher inference on all training data — one-time cost
Can pre-compute teacher logits and cache them — saves re-running teacher
Student training cost ≈ same as training from scratch (same dataset size, smaller model)

related: FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS — student can use different architecture than teacher

application_relevance: Positioning against GPT-4: 'too expensive and slow' solved by distilling into a task-specific smaller architecture at fraction of cost.
